notes: Official Monarch product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
For young homeowners with a child, most people start by protecting 3 things: (1) income, (2) the home, and (3) liability if something big goes wrong. I’ll walk through each type, what to prioritize, and common gaps to watch for.

Since I don’t have your exact details (income, ages, mortgage, etc.), I’ll keep this general. If you’d like, we can then size rough coverage amounts for your situation.

1. Life insurance (often the biggest gap)

Why it matters: Replaces income and covers the mortgage and childcare if one of you dies.

What many families consider:

Term life (e.g., 20–30 years) that lasts until:
The mortgage is mostly paid
Child(ren) are through college
Coverage amount often based on:
Remaining mortgage
Other debts not forgiven at death
Years of income you’d want replaced
Childcare and education costs
Final expenses, minus existing savings

Common gaps:

Only having small employer life insurance (1–2x salary) and nothing else
One partner uninsured or underinsured (especially a stay‑at‑home parent whose work would be expensive to replace)
Term length too short (e.g., 10 years when your child is 2 and mortgage is 30 years)

You could consider:

Listing out debts, income, and goals, then estimating a minimum and “ideal” coverage range for each adult.
2. Disability insurance (protecting your paycheck)

Why it matters: You’re statistically more likely to be disabled than to die during working years. Disability coverage replaces income if you can’t work.

What to look at:

Employer long‑term disability (LTD):
Benefit percentage (often 40–60% of salary)
Whether it covers bonus/commission
Waiting period (often 90 days)
“Own occupation” vs “any occupation” definition

Common gaps:

No LTD at all, or only short‑term disability
LTD that replaces too little income (e.g., 40% before tax)
Not realizing benefits are taxable if premiums are employer‑paid

You could consider:

Making sure each income‑earner has at least some LTD, and understanding how much of your current budget it would actually cover.
3. Homeowners insurance (protecting the house + liability)

Key pieces to review:

Dwelling coverage: Enough to rebuild the home, not just pay off the mortgage
Personal property: Are limits high enough for furniture, electronics, kids’ stuff, etc.?
Loss of use: Covers living elsewhere if your home is uninhabitable
Liability coverage: Protects if someone is injured on your property or you cause damage

Common gaps:

Dwelling limit not updated for higher construction costs
High deductibles you couldn’t comfortably pay
Low liability limits (e.g., $100K) when many families prefer higher (e.g., $300K–$500K+)
No special riders for valuables (jewelry, instruments, etc.) if needed

You could consider:

Asking your insurer what it would cost to increase liability and confirm replacement‑cost coverage (not actual cash value) on the structure and contents.
4. Liability / Umbrella insurance

Why it matters: Protects your income and assets if you’re sued for more than your auto/home policy limits.

Typical structure:

Umbrella policies usually start at $1M coverage and sit on top of:
Auto liability
Homeowners liability

Common gaps:

No umbrella coverage even though you:
Own a home
Have a child’s friends over
Drive regularly
Auto/home liability limits too low to qualify for an umbrella or to protect against a serious accident

You could consider:

Reviewing your current auto/home liability limits and asking what an extra $1M umbrella would cost (often surprisingly affordable).
5. Health insurance

Why it matters: One major medical event can derail finances.

Key items to review:

Network: Are your child’s pediatrician and your preferred providers in‑network?
Deductible and out‑of‑pocket max: What’s the worst‑case annual cost?
HSA eligibility (if on a high‑deductible plan): Can you use an HSA for tax‑advantaged medical savings?

Common gaps:

Not understanding the out‑of‑pocket maximum for a family
No plan for how to cover the deductible if something happens early in the year
Not using an HSA when eligible (if it fits your situation)

You could consider:

Making sure your emergency fund at least covers your family’s out‑of‑pocket max or a good portion of it.
6. Auto insurance

Key protections:

Liability: Bodily injury and property damage you cause to others
Uninsured/underinsured motorist: If the other driver is at fault but poorly insured
Collision/comprehensive: Damage to your own car

Common gaps:

State‑minimum liability limits that are far too low for a family with a home and future earnings to protect
Skimpy uninsured/underinsured coverage
Not updating mileage/usage (e.g., now driving less, or more, than when policy was set)

You could consider:

Checking that your liability limits are meaningfully above state minimums and aligned with your net worth and income potential.
7. Other protections to consider
Term life on each parent (even non‑earning) to cover childcare and household work
Basic estate planning:
Will
Guardianship designations for your child
Powers of attorney and healthcare directives
Renter’s insurance (if you ever rent out part of your home or move temporarily)
Flood/earthquake insurance if your area is at risk (often not included in standard homeowners policies)

If you’d like to go one level deeper, you could share:

Ages of adults, rough income, and mortgage balance
Whether you have any life or disability coverage through work now

Then Monarch can help you think through rough coverage ranges and which 1–2 changes might have the bigges
